volumeTitle: The Woman of Andros. The Self-Tormentor. The Eunuch
label: The Eunuch (Section front.cast - Section 1.2.130)

sections.label: Section front.cast
sections.original:
FABULAE INTERLOCUTORES .

ANTIPHO , adolescens . CHAEREA , adolescens . CHREMES , adolescens rusticus . DORIAS , ancilla . DORUS , eunuchus . GNATHO , parasitus . LACHES , senex . PARMENO , servus . PHAEDRIA , adolescens . PYTHIAS , ancilla . SANGA , lixa . SOPHRONA , nutrix . THAIS , meretrix . THRASO , miles .
sections.translation: Dramatis Personae LACHES , [*] an aged Athenian . PHAEDRIA , [*] his sons . CHAEREA [*] ANTIPHO , [*] a young man , friend of Chaerea . CHREMES , [*] a young man , brother of Pamphila . THRASO , [*] a boastful Captain . GNATHO , [*] a Parasite . PARMENO , [*] servant of Phldria . SANGA , [*] cook to Thraso . DONAX , [*] servants of Thraso . SIMALIO [*] SYRISCUS [*] DORUS , a Eunuch slave . THAIS , [*] a Courtesan . PYTHIAS , [*] her attendants . DORIAS [*] SOPHRONA , [*] a nurse . PAMPHILA , [*] a female slave .

sections.label: Section front.production
sections.original: EUNUCHUS . ACTA LUDIS MEGALENSIBUS , L . POSTUMIO ALBINO L . CORNELIO MERULA AEDILIBUS CURULIBUS . EGERE L . AMBIVIUS TURPIO L . ATILIUS PRAENESTINUS TIBIIS DUABUS DEXTRIS . GRAECA MENANDRU . ACTA II . MODOS FECIT FLACCUS CLAUDII , M . VALERIO C . FANNIO COSS .
sections.translation:
THE TITLE [*] OF THE PLAY .
PERFORMED at the Megalensian Games ; L . Posthumius Albinus and L . Cornelius Merula being Curule Aediles . L . Ambivius Turpio and L . Atilius Praenestinus performed it . Flaccus , the freedman of Claudius , composed the music to two treble flutes . From the Greek of Menander . It was acted twice , [*] M . Valerius and C . Fannius being Consuls . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.1
sections.original: Si quisquam est qui placere se studeat bonis
sections.translation: IF there is any one who desires to please as many good men as possible , and to give offense to extremely few , among those does our Poet enroll his name .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.4
sections.original: Tum si quis est qui dictum in se inclementius
sections.translation: Next , if there is one who thinks [*] that language too harsh is here applied to him , let him bear this in mind —

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.6
sections.original: Responsum non dictum esse , quia laesit prior ,
sections.translation: that it is an answer , not an attack ; inasmuch as he has himself been the first aggressor ; who , by translating plays verbally , [*] and writing them in bad Latin , has made out of good Greek Plays Latin ones by no means good . Just as of late he has published the Phasma [*] [ the Apparition ] of Menander ;

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.10
sections.original: Atque in Thesauro scripsit causamn dicere
sections.translation: and in the Thesaurus [ the Treasure ] has described [*] him from whom the gold is demanded , as pleading his cause why it should be deemed his own , before the person who demands it has stated how this treasure belongs to him , or how it came into the tomb of his father . Henceforward , let him not deceive himself , or fancy thus ,

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.15
sections.original: Defunctus iam sum , nihil est quod dicat mihi ;
sections.translation: I have now done with it ; there ’ s nothing that he can say to me . I recommend him not to be mistaken , and to refrain from provoking me . I have many other points , as to which for the present he shall be pardoned , which , however , shall be brought forward hereafter , if he persists in attacking me ,

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.19
sections.original: Ita ut facere instituit . Quam nunc acturi sumus
sections.translation: as he has begun to do . After the Aediles had purchased the Eunuch of Menander , the Play which we are about to perform , he managed to get an opportunity of viewing it . [*] When the magistrates were present it began to be performed . He exclaimed that a thief , no Poet , had produced the piece , but still had not deceived [*] him ;

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.25
sections.original: Colacem esse Naevi et Plauti veterem fabulam ;
sections.translation: that , in fact , it was the Colax , an old Play of Plautus ; [*] and that from it were taken the characters of the Parasite and the Captain . If this is a fault , the fault is the ignorance of the Poet ; not that he intended to be guilty of theft . That so it is , you will now be enabled to judge .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.30
sections.original: Colax Menandri est ; in ea est parasitus Colax ,
sections.translation: The Colax is a Play of Meander ’ s ; in it there is Colax , a Parasite , and a braggart Captain : he does not deny that he has transferred these characters into his Eunuch from the Greek ; but assuredly he does deny this , that he was aware that those pieces had been already translated into Latin .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.35
sections.original: Quod si personis iisdem uti aliis non licet ,
sections.translation: But if it is not permitted us to use the same characters as others , how can it any more be allowed to represent hurrying servants , [*] to describe virtuous matrons , artful courtesans , the gluttonous parasite , the braggart captain , the infant palmed off , the old man cajoled by the servant ,

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.40
sections.original: Amare , odisse , suspicari ? Denique
sections.translation: about love , hatred , suspicion ? In fine , nothing is said now that has not been said before . Wherefore it is but just that you should know this , and make allowance , if the moderns do what the ancients used to do . Grant me your attention , and give heed in silence ,

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.45
sections.original: Ut pernoscatis quid sibi Eunuchus velit .
sections.translation: that you may understand what the Eunuch means .

sections.label: Section 1.1.46
sections.original: Quid igitur faciam ? non eam ? ne nunc quidem
sections.translation: What then , shall I do ? [*] Ought I not to go , not now even , when I am sent for of her own accord ? Or ought I rather so to behave myself as not to put up with affronts from Courtesans ? She shut her door against me ; she now invites me back . Ought I to return ? No ; though she should implore me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: Si quidem hercle possis , nihil prius neque fortius :
sections.translation: l ’ faith , if indeed you only can , there ’ s nothing better or more spirited ; but if you begin , and can not hold out stoutly , and if , when you can not endure it , while no one asks you , peace being not made , you come to her of your own accord , showing that you love her , and can not endure it , you are done for ; it ’ s all over with you ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: Peristi . Eludet , ubi te victum senserit .
sections.translation: you are ruined outright . She ’ ll be jilting you , when she finds you overcome . Do you then , while there ’ s time , again and again reflect upon this , master , that a matter , which in itself admits of neither prudence nor moderation , you are unable to manage with prudence . In love there are all these evils ; wrongs ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.original: Suspiciones , inimicitiae , indutiae ,
sections.translation: suspicions , enmities reconcilements , war , then peace ; if you expect to render these things , naturally uncertain , certain by dint of reason , you wouldn ’ t effect it a bit the more than if you were to use your endeavors to be mad with reason . And , what you are now , in anger , meditating to yourself ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.original: Egone illam quae illum ? quae me ? quae non ? sine modo :
sections.translation: What ! I to her ? [*] Who — him ! Who — me ! Who wouldn ’ t ? Only let me alone ; I had rather die ; she shall find out what sort of a person I am ; these expressions , upon my faith , by a single false tiny tear , which , by rubbing her eyes , poor thing , she can hardly squeeze out perforce , she will put an end to ; and she ’ ll be the first to accuse you ; and you will be

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: Ei ultro supplicium .
sections.translation: too ready to give satisfaction to her .

sections.label: Section 1.1.70b
sections.original: O indignum facinus ! nunc ego et
sections.translation: O disgraceful conduct ! I now perceive , both that she is perfidious , and that I am a wretched man . I am both weary of her , and burn with passion ; knowing and fully sensible , alive and seeing it , I am going to ruin ; nor do I know what I am to do .

sections.label: Section 1.1.74
sections.original: Quid agas nisi ut te redimas captum quam queas
sections.translation: What you are to do ? Why , only to redeem yourself , thus captivated ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: Minimo ? si nequeas paululo , at quanti queas :
sections.translation: at the smallest price you can ; if you can not at a very small , rate , still for as little as you can ; and do not afflict yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.1.76b
sections.original: Itane suades ?
sections.translation: Do you persuade me to this ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.76c
sections.original: Si sapis :
sections.translation: If you are wise . And don ’ t be adding to the troubles which love itself produces ; those which it does produce , bear patiently . But see , here she is coming herself , the downfall of our fortunes , [*] —

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: Nam quod nos capere oportet haec intercipit .
sections.translation: for that which we ought ourselves to enjoy she intercepts .

sections.label: Section 1.2.81
sections.original: Miseram me ! vereor ne illud gravius Phaedria
sections.translation: Ah wretched me ! I fear lest Phaedria should take it amiss or otherwise than I intended it , that he was not admitted yesterday .

sections.label: Section 1.2.83b
sections.original: Totus Parmeno
sections.translation: I ’ m trembling and shivering all over , Parmeno , at the sight of her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.84b
sections.original: Bono animo es :
sections.translation: Be of good heart ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.85
sections.original: Accede ad ignem hunc ; iam calesces plus satis .
sections.translation: only approach this fire , [*] you ’ ll soon be warmer than you need .

sections.label: Section 1.2.86
sections.original: Quis hic loquitur ? ehem , tune hic eras , mi Phaedria ?
sections.translation: Who is it that ’ s speaking here ? What , are you here , my Phaedria ? Why are you standing here ? Why didn ’ t you come into the house at once ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.87b
sections.original: Caeterum
sections.translation: But not a word about shutting you out !

sections.label: Section 1.2.88b
sections.original: Quid taces ?
sections.translation: Why are you silent ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.89
sections.original: Sane quia vero hae mihi patent semper fores ,
sections.translation: Of course , it ’ s because [*] this door is always open to me ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.90
sections.original: Aut quia sum apud te primus .
sections.translation: or because I ’ m the highest in your favor ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.90b
sections.original: Missa istaec face .
sections.translation: Pass those matters by .

sections.label: Section 1.2.91
sections.original: Quid missa ? o Thais , Thais , utinam esset mihi
sections.translation: How pass them by ? O Thais , Thais , I wish that I had equal affection with yourself , and that it were in like degree , that either this might distress you in the same way that it distresses me , or that I might be indifferent at this being done by you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.95
sections.original: Ne crucia te , obsecro , anime mi , mi Phaedria .
sections.translation: Prithee , don ’ t torment yourself , my life , my Phaedria . Upon my faith , I did it , not because I love or esteem any person more than you ; but the case was such that it was necessary to be done .

sections.label: Section 1.2.98
sections.original: Credo ut fit misera prae amore exclusti hunc foras .
sections.translation: I suppose that , poor thing , you shut him out of doors , for love , according to the usual practice .

sections.label: Section 1.2.99
sections.original: Sicine ais Parmeno ? age : sed huc qua gratia
sections.translation: Is it thus you act , Parmeno ? Well , well . ( To PHAEDRIA .) But listen — the reason for which

sections.label: Section 1.2.100
sections.original: Te arcessi iussi ausculta .
sections.translation: I desired you to be sent for hither —

sections.label: Section 1.2.100b
sections.original: Fiat .
sections.translation: Go on .

sections.label: Section 1.2.100c
sections.original: Dic mihi
sections.translation: First tell me this ; can this fellow possibly hold his tongue ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.101b
sections.original: Egone ? optime .
sections.translation: What , I ? Perfectly well . But , hark you , upon these conditions I pledge my word to you ; the truth that I hear , I ’ m silent upon , and retain it most faithfully ; but if I hear what ’ s false and without foundation , it ’ s out at once ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.105
sections.original: Plenus rimaraum sum ; hac atque illac perfluo .
sections.translation: I ’ m full of chinks , and leak in every direction . Therefore , if you wish it to be kept secret , speak the truth .

sections.label: Section 1.2.107
sections.original: Samia mihi mater fuit : ea habitabat Rhodi .
sections.translation: My mother was a Samian ; she lived at Rhodes —

sections.label: Section 1.2.108
sections.original: Potest taceri hoc .
sections.translation: That may be kept a secret .

sections.label: Section 1.2.108b
sections.original: Ibi tum matri parvulam
sections.translation: There , at that period , a certain merchant made present to my mother of a little girl ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: Ex Attica hine abreptam .
sections.translation: who had been stolen away from Attica here .

sections.label: Section 1.2.110b
sections.original: Civemne ?
sections.translation: What , a citizen ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.110c
sections.original: Arbitror :
sections.translation: I think so ; we do not know for certain : she herself used to mention her mother ’ s and her father ’ s name ; her country and other tokens she didn ’ t know , nor , by reason of her age , was she able . The merchant added this : that he had heard from the kidnappers

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: Unde emerat se audisse abreptam e Sunio .
sections.translation: that she had been carried off from Sunium . [*] When my mother received her , she began carefully to teach her every thing , and to bring her up , just as though she had been her own daughter . Most persons supposed that she was my sister .

sections.label: Section 1.2.119
sections.original: Ego cum illo quicum tum uno rem habebam hospite
sections.translation: Thence I came hither with that stranger , with whom alone at that period I was connected ; he left me all which I now possess —

sections.label: Section 1.2.121
sections.original: Utrumque hoc falsum est : effluet .
sections.translation: Both these things are false ; out it goes .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121b
sections.original: Qui istuc ?
sections.translation: How so ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.121c
sections.original: Quia
sections.translation: Because you were neither content with one , nor was he the only one to make you presents ; for he likewise ( pointing to PHAEDRIA ) brought a pretty considerable share to you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.124
sections.original: Ita est : sed sine me pervenire quo volo .
sections.translation: Such is the fact ; but do allow me to arrive at the point I wish .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: Interea miles qui me amare occeperat
sections.translation: In the mean time , the Captain , who had begun to take a fancy to me , set out to Caria ; [*] since when , in the interval , I became acquainted with you . You yourself are aware how very dear I have held you ; and how I confess to you all my nearest counsels .

sections.label: Section 1.2.129
sections.original: Ne hoc quidem tacebit Parmeno .
sections.translation: Nor will Parmeno be silent about that .

sections.label: Section 1.2.129b
sections.original: Oh , dubiumne id est ?
sections.translation: O , is that a matter of doubt ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: Hoc agite amabo . Mater mea illic mortua est
sections.translation: Attend ; I entreat you . My mother died there recently ; her brother is somewhat greedy after wealth . When he saw that this damsel was of beauteous form and understood music , hoping for a good price , he forthwith put her up for sale , and sold her . By good fortune
